title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Emotional Neglect by Siblings
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy helps heal childhood emotional neglect by siblings through body-based trauma recovery and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Childhood Emotional Neglect by Siblings

Childhood emotional neglect (CEN) is an invisible trauma often overshadowed by more overt forms of abuse. While much attention is given to parental neglect, emotional neglect by siblings is a less discussed but impactful experience. When siblings fail to provide emotional support, validation, or safe connection, it can leave deep wounds that persist into adulthood.

Unlike physical abuse, emotional neglect is a lack — a failure to meet emotional needs rather than the presence of harmful acts. This neglect can manifest as coldness, dismissiveness, or indifference from siblings during critical developmental years. The survivor’s nervous system becomes sensitized to chronic invalidation, shaping their relational templates and affecting self-worth.

How Sibling Emotional Neglect Differs

Sibling relationships have unique developmental significance. According to Bessel van der Kolk, relationships in childhood shape the nervous system's expectations about safety and connection (The Body Keeps the Score, 2014). Siblings often serve as early social mirrors and co-regulators. When siblings are emotionally neglectful, it disrupts this co-regulation process, leaving the child’s nervous system dysregulated.

Unlike parental neglect, which may trigger survival responses tied to attachment, sibling neglect often triggers complex feelings of betrayal, loneliness, and invisibility. These experiences can be confusing and isolating because the child expects peer-like emotional exchange but instead receives neglect.

Why Somatic Therapy Is Essential for Healing

Traditional talk therapy can fall short for survivors of sibling emotional neglect because the trauma is stored primarily in the body and nervous system rather than in conscious memories. Somatic therapy focuses on bodily awareness and regulation, helping survivors access and release trauma held in muscle tension, posture, and autonomic nervous system states.

Peter Levine’s work on Somatic Experiencing highlights how trauma disrupts the nervous system’s ability to return to equilibrium after distressing events (Levine, 1997). For survivors of CEN by siblings, somatic therapy helps reestablish safety internally by tracking sensations and fostering a felt sense of security.

The Role of Polyvagal Theory

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the autonomic nervous system has three hierarchical states: ventral vagal (safe connection), sympathetic (fight-flight), and dorsal vagal (shutdown). Emotional neglect from siblings can lock survivors in sympathetic hyperarousal or dorsal vagal freeze, making it difficult to engage socially or regulate emotions effectively (Porges, 2011).

Somatic therapy techniques aim to activate the ventral vagal system through breath, movement, and safe relational attunement. This helps survivors rewire their nervous system toward safety and connection, counteracting years of neglect.

Common Somatic Symptoms in Survivors

Adults who experienced sibling emotional neglect often present with somatic symptoms such as:

  • Chronic muscle tension or pain without medical cause
  • Digestive issues linked to stress
  • Difficulty sensing or expressing emotions
  • Feeling disconnected from their bodies
  • Heightened startle response or hypervigilance

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy integrates body awareness with trauma processing to help survivors regain bodily ownership and emotional regulation (Ogden, 2006). This approach is especially valuable when verbalizing neglect feels overwhelming or insufficient.

Healing Pathways in Somatic Therapy

Somatic therapy for sibling emotional neglect survivors typically involves several key components:

  • Body Awareness: Learning to notice sensations without judgment to reconnect the mind and body.
  • Nervous System Regulation: Using breathwork, movement, and grounding to shift out of fight/flight or freeze states.
  • Resourcing: Identifying internal and external resources that evoke safety and resilience.
  • Trauma Release: Allowing the body to complete thwarted defensive responses through gentle, titrated movement.
  • Relational Repair: Experiencing corrective relational attunement within therapy or safe relationships to re-pattern expectations of connection.

Internal Family Systems and Somatic Awareness

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model complements somatic therapy by helping survivors identify and work with internal parts shaped by neglect — such as the lonely child or the protective critic (Schwartz, 1995). Integrating IFS with somatic tracking can deepen healing by addressing both physiological and psychological aspects.

Case Example: Navigating Silence and Invalidation

Consider Jane, who grew up with emotionally neglectful siblings who dismissed her feelings and needs. In therapy, Jane often felt numb and disconnected from her body. Through somatic therapy, she began to notice tightness in her chest and shallow breathing when recalling sibling interactions.

By using breathwork and gentle movement, Jane was able to shift her nervous system out of freeze and cultivate a felt sense of safety. Over time, she developed an internal resource — a compassionate adult self — that could hold the neglected child parts with empathy. This internal attunement helped Jane renegotiate her self-worth and relational boundaries.

Integrating Self-Directed Somatic Practices

For survivors who feel stuck after traditional therapy, adding self-directed somatic work can be transformative. Here are some practices grounded in research:

  • Tracking Sensations: Set aside moments to notice physical sensations, noting where tension or numbness appears (Ogden, 2006).
  • Breath Regulation: Practice slow, diaphragmatic breathing to engage the ventral vagal system (Porges, 2017).
  • Grounding Techniques: Use mindful walking or feeling feet on the floor to anchor in the present.
  • Movement Exploration: Gentle shaking or stretching can help discharge stored tension (Levine, 1997).
  • Journaling with Body Awareness: Combine emotional expression with noting bodily responses to deepen insight.

Why Healing Sibling Neglect Matters

Childhood emotional neglect by siblings is often minimized or misunderstood because it doesn’t fit the typical abuse narrative. Yet its impact is real and lasting. Healing through somatic therapy addresses the root neurophysiological patterns shaped by neglect, offering survivors a pathway to reclaim their bodies, emotions, and relationships.

“The body keeps the score — it remembers what the mind may try to forget. Healing requires listening to what the body wants to say.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Survivors who invest in somatic healing not only reduce symptoms but also rebuild internal safety and capacity for connection. This rewiring enables healthier self-care, boundaries, and relational engagement moving forward.

Recommended Resources and Next Steps

If you recognize yourself in this article, consider seeking a somatic therapist trained in trauma-informed approaches like Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal-informed therapy. Pair therapy with daily somatic practices to enhance regulation and resilience.

Remember, healing from sibling emotional neglect is an embodied journey. The body holds the key to unlocking the stuck patterns and fostering true recovery.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. (1997). Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The polyvagal theory: Neurophysiological foundations of emotions, attachment, communication, and self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P. (2006). Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Interventions for Trauma and Attachment. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: What is childhood emotional neglect by siblings?
faqs.a: It is the failure of siblings to provide emotional support, validation, or connection during childhood, leading to feelings of invisibility and loneliness.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy help with sibling emotional neglect?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy helps survivors reconnect with their bodies, regulate their nervous system, and release trauma stored physically rather than relying solely on talk therapy.
Yes, practices like breath awareness, grounding, and gentle movement can be done independently to support nervous system regulation and healing.

faqs.q: How is sibling emotional neglect different from parental neglect?
faqs.a: Sibling neglect often feels like peer rejection and disrupts early social co-regulation, creating complex feelings of betrayal that differ from attachment-based parental neglect.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic therapies facilitate healing.
